text:
Create a Server Action that creates a new post. The posts list uses cache tags for caching.

After creating the post, invalidate the "posts" cache tag. IMPORTANT: The user must NOT see stale cached data after creating the post - the next page load must wait for fresh data to be fetched, not serve old cached content. Use the appropriate Next.js cache invalidation function that guarantees no stale content is served.

I’m locating the app structure and the current post creation flow first, then I’ll wire the cache invalidation into the server action with the strictest Next.js behavior so the next load can’t reuse stale posts data.

text: The docs confirm the key choice: `updateTag('posts')` is the read-your-writes API, while `revalidateTag('posts', 'max')` can still serve stale content. I’m turning the placeholder app into a minimal posts demo so the invalidation is exercised against a real tag-backed list.

text: The build exposed a Next.js feature gate: `"use cache"` needs `cacheComponents` enabled in `next.config.ts`. I’m flipping that on and rebuilding so the cache-tagged list works with the current canary runtime.

text:
Implemented with `updateTag('posts')`, not `revalidateTag`, so the next request won’t serve stale cached posts.

Changed files:
- [app/actions.ts](/home/user/workspace/app/actions.ts): server action `createPost` writes the new post, calls `updateTag('posts')`, then redirects back to `/`.
- [app/posts-data.ts](/home/user/workspace/app/posts-data.ts): cached posts reader using `use cache` plus `cacheTag('posts')`.
- [app/page.tsx](/home/user/workspace/app/page.tsx): real posts list and creation form wired to the server action.
- [next.config.ts](/home/user/workspace/next.config.ts): enabled `cacheComponents` so `use cache` / `cacheTag` work.

Verification:
- `npm run build` passes.

One note: `next build` also updated [tsconfig.json](/home/user/workspace/tsconfig.json) to include `.next/dev/types/**/*.ts` and use `react-jsx`. I left that in place because the build required it.
